title: St. Constantine the Emperor, St. Helena, St. Hilarion of Pelekete & St. Stephen the Wonderworker
narrative:
St. Constantine the Great Equal to the Apostles, St. Helena the Empress, St. Hilarion of Pelekete, and St. Stephen the Wonderworker

On the twenty-eighth of Megabit, the holy Church celebrates an assembly of four witnesses who are united by the theme of imperial and royal holiness — the sanctification not of the desert cell or the martyr's arena but of the throne, the palace, and the structures of worldly power that the Gospel does not destroy but transforms from within. Saint Constantine the Great stands at the head of this assembly as the most consequential of all the imperial saints: the emperor whose conversion — however its exact nature is debated by historians — decisively changed the conditions of Christian life throughout the Roman world, ending the era of persecution, convening the Council of Nicaea, and establishing the legal framework within which the Church could develop its institutional life. The Ethiopian tradition honours Constantine not uncritically — the problems of the Constantinian settlement are not invisible to a church that has itself navigated the relationship between faith and imperial power — but with the recognition that his conversion was, whatever its mixed motives, the occasion of an extraordinary providential development.

Saint Helena, his mother, is in many respects the more straightforwardly holy of the two: a woman who received her son's faith not merely as a political conversion but as a personal and transformative encounter with the living God, who spent her final years on pilgrimage to the Holy Land, distributing enormous wealth to the poor, building churches on the sacred sites, and performing the excavations that led to the Finding of the True Cross already commemorated in this month. Helena's piety was practical in the most literal sense — she built, she gave, she dug, she found. The tradition honours her as the model of the lay Christian of means whose wealth is placed wholly at the service of the Church and the poor.

Saint Hilarion of Pelekete returns in this assembly — already commemorated on the twenty-fifth — because his feast is also associated with the theme of imperial-monastic conflict: his monastery's destruction under the Iconoclast emperor Leo III places him in the long tradition of monastic resistance to imperial overreach in religious matters, a tradition that both the Byzantine and the Ethiopian churches know from their own histories. The Iconoclast controversy is in some ways the Christian version of the question that Constantine resolved in the opposite direction: what happens when the emperor uses his power not to free the Church but to constrain it, not to build churches but to destroy images? Hilarion is the answer — the monk who continues to pray in the ruins of the monastery, whose witness outlasts the emperor's power to suppress it.

Saint Stephen the Wonderworker of Constantinople is this day's discovery: a monk of the Constantinopolitan ascetic tradition who was known from his youth for a gift of healing and wonder that drew the sick and desperate from across the city to his small monastery. He survived the first phase of the Iconoclast controversy, navigating it with diplomatic skill, and continued his ministry of healing and intercession until an advanced age. The tradition notes that Stephen never performed miracles ostentatiously but with a kind of matter-of-fact compassion, as though the healing were the natural response to the need and required no extraordinary effort on his part — a quality the tradition associates with those in whom divine compassion has become, through long prayer, the dominant mode of being. Constantine who changed the world, Helena who found the Cross, Hilarion who held the monastery against the emperor, Stephen who healed quietly in his cell: four aspects of the holiness that does not flee the world but redeems it. Amen.
